topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical weights pressing down on Elias. He stood by the stark white canvas, his hands clenched so tightly his knuckles were white moons against the dark fabric. When he finally saw it—his latest piece, a swirling depiction of fractured light—hanging where his signature should have been, a cold, hollow ache settled deep in his chest. He didn't move, just stared at the intrusion, the unfamiliar scrutiny of strangers’ eyes making the air feel thin and suffocating. A low, ragged sound escaped his throat, more of a whisper than a complaint, and he turned away, the silence of the empty studio amplifying the strange, unwelcome spotlight on his failure.

[story 2]

"It’s… not mine," Clara murmured, her voice brittle, tracing the edge of the framed sculpture with a fingertip as if it might dissolve. She kept her gaze fixed on the piece, her shoulders slumped forward, a visible surrender to the absurdity of the situation. Every breath she took seemed shallow, catching painfully in her throat. She shuffled her feet on the polished floor, the sound loud in the hushed space, a restless energy battling a profound sense of being utterly displaced. The tension radiating off her was palpable, a tight wire ready to snap under the pressure of the unsolicited display.

[story 3]

I walked into the room and stopped dead. The air immediately felt thick, heavy, like breathing underwater. My gaze swept over the arrangement—my work, undeniably, yet utterly divorced from my intent. A dull, heavy pressure settled behind my eyes, making the edges of the room blur slightly. I didn't speak; the words seemed lodged somewhere behind my teeth. I ran a hand over my face, feeling the slight tremor beneath my skin, a physical manifestation of the sheer unfairness of it all. I simply stood there, rooted to the spot, feeling utterly drained, like a battery that had been completely depleted.
